title: Accounts Payable Simulation
subhead: You're the new AP clerk at a $2M/year Virginia manufacturer. Thirty real vendor invoices just landed on your desk. Some are clean. Some are wrong. Your job is to know the difference before anything gets paid.
hook: "Walk me through how you catch a bad invoice before it goes out the door." — this is one of the most common questions asked in AP clerk interviews. By the end of this module you'll have a real answer, with real numbers.

chunks.title: The AP process: receive → verify → approve → enter → pay
chunks.bodyHtml:
Every invoice that arrives at Skarion Manufacturing goes through the same five steps before a payment ever leaves the bank account:

  1. Receive — the invoice arrives (mail, email, vendor portal).
  2. Verify — match it against the Purchase Order (PO) and the Receiving Report. This is called the 3-way match.
  3. Approve — if everything matches, it's approved for entry. If not, it gets held.
  4. Enter — the bill is entered into QuickBooks, coded to the right expense account.
  5. Pay — on the vendor's terms (Net 30, etc.), the payment goes out.

The 3-way match is the control that catches almost everything that goes wrong in AP: wrong prices, wrong quantities, invoices with no matching PO at all, and duplicate invoices vendors accidentally (or not-so-accidentally) send twice.

callout.html: Interview tip: "3-way match" is one of the single most-asked technical phrases in AP/staff accountant interviews. If you can define it AND give a real example of catching a discrepancy with it, you're ahead of most candidates.

chunks.title: Know your discrepancy types
chunks.intro: Flip each card to check yourself before you start processing invoices.

cards.back: The invoice charges a different unit price than the PO. Example: PO says $38.00/unit for aluminum, invoice bills $39.50/unit.
The invoice bills for more units than were actually received. Example: billed for 500 units, receiving report shows only 450 arrived.
A materials invoice over $500 with no PO number attached — held until someone confirms the purchase was authorized.
Same invoice number, same PO, same amount as one already entered. Vendors resend these by accident (or send a 'statement copy') — never pay twice.
A legitimate invoice with an added line — like a rush fee — that was never on the original PO. Needs approval before it's paid.
Recurring service invoices (rent, utilities, software subscriptions) don't need a PO — they're approved once and paid on a set schedule.

chunks.title: The batch on your desk: 30 invoices, 22 vendors
chunks.bodyHtml:
This is real production data from the Skarion Manufacturing simulation — not made-up numbers. Every dollar amount, PO number, and discrepancy below is something you'll actually process in the exercise.

VendorInvoiceIssue
Dominion Aluminum & FastenersDAF-2601-014Price discrepancy: PO $38.00/unit, billed $39.50/unit
Blue Ridge Industrial PlasticsBRIP-2601-006Quantity discrepancy: billed 500, received 450
NOVA Packaging & CratingNPC-2601-011Missing PO — invoice held for approval
Potomac Steel SupplyPSS-2601-001 (again as #021)Duplicate invoice — vendor sent a statement copy
Commonwealth Powder CoatingCPC-2601-003Unauthorized rush fee added, not on the PO

All 30 invoices' math has already been verified to the penny — your job in the exercise isn't to find math errors, it's to catch the process errors above.

callout.html: One extra realistic trap in this batch: there are two different companies with "NOVA" in the name — NOVA Packaging & Crating and NOVA Industrial Waste. They are not the same vendor. Real AP clerks get tripped up by this constantly — always check the full vendor name and ID, not just the first word.

chunks.title: Virginia sales tax: goods vs. services
chunks.bodyHtml:
Virginia charges 5.3% sales tax on tangible goods — but not on most services. This matters constantly in AP: an invoice can have both a materials line (taxed) and a labor/service line (not taxed) on the same invoice.

Example from Potomac Steel Supply's invoice: a "cutting surcharge" service line and a raw steel materials line appear together — only the steel is taxed at 5.3%. Get this wrong and your AP totals won't reconcile to the vendor's actual invoice.

questions.q: A vendor invoice for $2,400 in raw materials arrives. The PO on file says $2,200. What's this called, and what do you do first?
options: A duplicate invoice — reject it outright
A price or quantity discrepancy — verify against the PO and receiving report before approving
A missing PO — hold indefinitely with no further action
Nothing to check — just pay the invoice as billed
questions.explanation: This is a discrepancy (price or quantity — you'd check the receiving report to know which). The correct move is always to verify against the PO/receiving report before approval, not to reject or auto-pay.

questions.q: Two invoices arrive with the same invoice number, same PO, and same dollar amount, a few weeks apart. What should you do?
options: Pay both — the vendor must need the cash
Flag it as a possible duplicate and confirm before paying a second time
Automatically increase the PO amount to match
Ignore it — invoice numbers don't matter
questions.explanation: Matching invoice number + PO + amount is the classic signature of a duplicate invoice (often an innocent vendor 'statement copy'). Flag and confirm before paying twice.

questions.q: An invoice has a $1,200 materials line and a $300 installation-labor line. Virginia sales tax is 5.3%. Which line(s) get taxed?
options: Neither line — services never get invoiced with tax
questions.explanation: Virginia taxes tangible goods, not services. The $1,200 materials line is taxed at 5.3% ($63.60); the $300 labor line is not.

chunks.title: Post the price-discrepancy resolution
chunks.scenarioHtml: Dominion Aluminum & Fasteners invoice DAF-2601-014 was approved with the vendor's corrected price after negotiation. You're recording the approved $3,896.33 materials bill (no sales complications — this is a straightforward accrual of an approved AP bill). Post the journal entry to record the bill as a liability.

chunks.title: Process the full 30-invoice batch
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Now do the real work. Open the invoice control log and the combined invoice PDF below. For each of the 30 invoices:

  1. 3-way match it against the PO/receiving data on the Student PO-Receiving Data tab.
  2. Decide: approve, hold, or flag as duplicate.
  3. Check your decisions against the Instructor Key tab once you've been through all 30.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Pick ONE invoice from this batch you'd use as your interview story. What was wrong with it, and what did you do?
